comment_content:
It mainly covers four parts: digital circuit, data representation, MIPS programming, processor.
If you have read the textbook (computer organization and design: the hardware/software interface), you would find Cindy tries to cover most of important knowledge. However, there still have some content she didn't mention, such as exception handling, multicycle pipeline, dynamic branch predication, inter x86/ARM instruction set. But I have to say this course is still rich. If you want to learn how computer works, this course would be a good choice.
comment_teaching: Cindy has tried to use many examples, such as library and washing machine, to help us understand some abstract concept, such as cache and pipeline. Though the PPT was badly designed, her course is good and I think you can fully understand the material if you attend the lecture.
comment_workload: The workload is appropriate. You only have 4 homework and 1 project. I think the project can be finished in 3 days, which is not much for a project!
comment_grading:
The grading scheme is not such good. However, with the bonus part, the overall grading is better.

Grading Scheme: (official)
Condition to Obtain A:
If your course overall is larger that mean+2sd, or your course overall is larger that 90, you would get an A. (if your course overall is larger than 95, you would definitely get A+.)
In general, mean is C+, mean+1sd is B, mean+2sd is A.
Bonus Part:
You have bonus part in project. At most you can add 2.25 points to your overall score. Cindy would first cut the letter grade without the bonus mark (i.e. >87 get A-). Then they would add the bonus to your overall score to check whether your grade would change. (i.e. you get 86, then you would receive B+ without bonus part. however, with the bonus, you would get A-, which is very good.)

comment_content: Refer to other reviews. : )
comment_teaching:
I think Cindy's teaching is quite nice. I like the way she includes simple metaphors in her teachings to help us better understand the content.

The content itself isn't particularly hard, but the volume and amount of content is actually quite overloading. Moreover, the content of each lecture is built onto of each other, meaning if you missed one lecture, you will have no clue what the current lecture is talking about.

As for the TA, I think Gary is has did quite a good job at the tutorials. He managed to explain the concepts clearly and generally the tutorials are alright.
comment_workload:
The 4 homeworks are extremely easy, mostly based on the questions already mentioned in the lectures and tutorials, shouldn't take much time to finish them.

There is 1 programming assignment, our's is a replica of the game "Super Mario Bros". Had lots of fun developing it and I think the TAs did a great job at creating this PA.
comment_grading:
Grading is absolutely crap.

Got 90 for mid term, 89 for finals, full in programming assignment and bonuses. Got 94.7 overall with bonus, only A. Sad.

comment_teaching:
我认为Cindy是一个热诚、负责的教授，但她的教学实在让人不敢恭维。我觉得计组最大的一个难点在于知识章节散，如果讲授者不能引导学生构建知识章节之间的联系，那么学生很容易陷入一种“局部理解但全局混沌”的困境。我认为一个好的计组老师应该告诉学生工程师们出于什么样的需要，并如何通过设计出的产品来满足这个需求，而不是直接告诉学生我们有这个东西。同时Cindy的讲课节奏和课件笔记很一般。

comment_content:
Most enjoyable course I have ever taken in UST up to yr-2, basically because computer organization itself is full of fun for me. Discovering the underlying logics behind programs is like exploring a brand new world. But some of my friends have different opinions, so it really depends on your own interest.

comment_teaching:
Many of my friends find Cindy's teaching boring and tedious, but somehow her teaching style suits me well.

She manages the pace well, and delivers the lectures in a well-organized way. Compared with Alex, she doesn't even waste a single words in explaining stuff. But you need to pay full attention to follow her.

Cindy is also willing to answer all your questions after class.

comment_grading:
According to Cindy, this course gives 25% A range, which is the largest possible to offer for a UG COMP course. But students in this course are so smart and competitive, and exam questions can be strange sometimes (in my sem, it is midterm to be strange) with a really tight time to finish. Small careless mistakes can be a disaster.

I got overall between 87 and 88 (just around UQ) but still B+. A friend with overall about 89 got A, so I guess Cindy gives more A than A-.

That said, she is a kind person and she is willing to answer your questions after class.

comment_content:
Syllabus:
(1) Introduction to Digital Logic
- Logic Gates, Truth Functions, Logic Function
- Combinational Logic
1. Multiplexor, decoder, PLA
2. Minterm / Maxterm, Canonical Forms
3. Boolean Algebra
4. K-map
- Sequential Logic
1. S-R latches
2. Gated D-Latch, Master-slave D Flip-flop
(2) Data Representation
- Signed and Unsigned Integer (2's complement)
- Floating Point Numbers (Single-Precision and Double-Precision IEEE754 Floating Point Standard)
- Characters
(3) MIPS ISA and Assembly
- Instruction Set Architecture, MIPS, Registers
- Endianness
- MIPS basic instructions
- Implementation of MIPS Instructions (R-format, I-format)
- Addressing modes
- Procedure
(4) Computer Arithmetic
- 2's Complement Arithmetic
- Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)
- Multiplication
- Division
(5) Processor
- Single-cycle Datapath
- Single-cycle Control
- MIPS Pipeline
- Pipeline Control
- Pipeline Hazards (Forwarding, Stall)
(6) Memory Hierarchy
- Memory
- Principle of Locality
- Cache

In general, we will deal with some digital logical circuit, then we deal with calculating numbers in binary and writing instructions in MIPS assembly language. Finally, we implement MIPS in processor and memory.
All of these will take some time understanding. It is best if you don't skip any lectures or at least spend some time every week to catch up.
comment_teaching:
Her teaching is decent. Her notes were quite helpful in understanding.
TA's notes were quite useful, and I used them as good exercise.
comment_workload:
Workload is decent. Each homework should take at most 1 day to do. Programming assignments should take at most 3 days (1 days for doing all, 2 days for debugging)
Both mid-term and final exam didn't take too much revision to do. However, final exam was a bit hard and sometimes ask you to add new stuff that we have never seen. (I don't think I have seen those questions in past papers) Therefore, it is pretty hard to achieve A range because mistakes can hurt a lot.

Note that I skipped most lectures and just watched the lecture videos in 2x speed, but I still found Cindy's teaching to be engaging.

--

The main IA, Anh Dung, as well as the rest of the TAs, clearly put a lot of effort into the labs and tutorials. Shoutout to Anh Dung for putting effort into them when the attendance (via Zoom) is in the single-digits.
comment_workload:
In terms of required workload, it is very low, despite it being a 4 credit course.

There are only four homeworks which usually takes a few hours at most.

The programming project is a bit tricky (and annoying), but it can be completed in a few days.

However, I would always recommend attending/watching the weekly lab and tutorials, because they contain some genuinely useful information and questions that can help reinforce your knowledge. I would also recommend you to spend some extra time playing around in Logisim and making circuit diagrams to understand things.
comment_grading:
Homework (x4) 15%
Project 15%
Midterm 30%
Final 40%

The course is very competitive, but I think the instructors are trying to give better grades every year. Mean seems to be a B/B-, and if you can get above 90%, it seems you can get A-range.

My suggestion is to always attend the paper checking session. The TAs marked both our exams within 12 hours of us taking it, but as a result, the marking is sloppy and contains many mistakes (they even added up my score wrong for the final). Also, the marking scheme may contain mistakes and/or not handle unorthodox but correct answers.

The exams are of acceptable difficulty, but some questions are quite tricky and hard to understand. It's also easy to make careless mistakes. Otherwise, the main difficulty is the time limit, which is 2 hours instead of the usual 3.

comment_teaching:
Teaching was decent - even though I skipped all the lectures, the lecture videos at 1.5x to 2x were very clear. I personally preferred Cindy's videos despite her accent - the examples she gave before diving into the slides helped illustrate what she is talking about. comment_workload:
Workload is generally quite low throughout the course.
There are four homeworks, each taking up a few hours, the midterm/final to review for, and the course project.

Probably the most annoying thing is the project - they modified the MARS IDE, but introduced quite a few bugs which would hang and/or crash the IDE if you wrote something improperly. Debugging assembly is a pain, especially with somewhat unreliable tools.

You are advised to do more practice with digital logic especially for the midterm.
comment_grading:
The grading scheme is as follows:
15% Homework (4 Sets)
15% Programming Project
30% Midterm
40% Final

From my (subjective) point of view, grading is better for the B range in general. Even though I scored below mean in the midterm and slightly above mean in the final, I still got a B. I do not have enough data to judge whether the grading is better for the A range though.

comment_content:
1. Digital Logic : it's my first time learning logic gates and using them to perform various operations. Before exam, I  recommend you prepare some tricks to simplify complicated boolean expressions.

2. Data Representation : pretty interesting to see how data are represented at low level like using IEEE 754 scheme and 2's complement. Also  here you will deal with bit array all the time so before exam, I  recommend you prepare some tricks to quickly manipulate powers of 2 e.g. \sum_{k=0}^{n-1}2^k = 2^{n}-1 (tho this actually comes from geometric series).  

3.  Instruction Set Architecture (ISA) and MIPS Assembly
     Language : pretty interesting to learn Assembly
     Language, it lets you experience a new style of programming. Before exam, I recommend you to equip yourself with ability to read & understand a block of MIPS code without any hint, you should be able to tell what the code is doing precisely.

4. Computer Arithmetic : here you'll learn ALU and how to implement arithmetic like multiplication & division with ALU. Before exam, I recommend you familiarize yourself with the every step of those algorithms including the states of the operands.  

5. Processor and pipeline : here you learn to implement various MIPS instructions with hardware. Before exam, I recommend you add additional hardware to the processor and implement more MIPS instructions, just play around.

6. Memory System : we only learned up to directed mapped cache this semester. Dr. Lam said if there's one thing that you should take away from COMP2611, then it has to the principle of locality (temporal and spatial). This principle is applied everywhere in CS and even other fields.
comment_teaching:
Dr. Lam always explain complicated topics in a way you can understand, clearly he has tons of experience teaching this course, he'll carry you.

The TA designing the project also seems to be pretty hard working as he answered questions on Piazza very fast.

The homework and assignments are also well designed.
comment_workload:
Pretty light. 4 homework, 1 PA, mid term and final exam. But for homework if you want good grade I recommend you triple check before submitting as you can safely assume everyone is going to get full mark and you cannot afford any careless mistake.

But I give B because of mental pressure created by the immense competition in this course.

Course Description
Inner workings of modern digital computer systems and tradeoffs at the hardware-software interface. Topics include: instructions set design, memory systems, input-output systems, interrupts and exceptions, pipelining, performance and cost analysis, assembly language programming, and a survey of advanced architectures.

comment_content:
1. Introduction to Digital Logic
Logic gates, Combinational and Sequential logic circuits, Boolean algebra & K-map

2. Data Representation
Signed & unsigned integers and floats using 2's complement and IEEE754

3. MIPS Assembly Language
Arithmetic operations, Control flow statements & functions,  Instruction formats, Recursion, Preserving registers

4. Arithmetic for Computers
Arithmetic Logic Unit and Integer Multiplication & Division

5. MIPS Processor
Single-cycle datapath & control and Pipelined Processor

6. Memory Hierarchy
Principle of Locality and Cache
comment_teaching: I really enjoy Professor Cindy's teaching. Overall, I feel that this is the easiest year 2 required comp course (as probably seen on the mean on the exams). But probably you need to think fast and avoid making careless mistakes as it's going to be costly if you are aiming for A-range. Grading is quite stingy too.
comment_workload: Workload is considered vv low for a 4 credit course imo (Nothing compared to COMP 2012 which has labs and PAs). However, you do need to prepare well for exams. comment_content:
Six major topics:
1. Digital Logic (Boolean Algebra, Different Logic Gates, PLA, etc)
2. Data Representation (2's complement for int, IEEE754 for float, ASCII for char, etc)
3. MIPS ISA and Assembly (essentially just MIPS Programming)
4. Computer Arithmetic (ALU, addition, subtraction, optimized multiplication, optimized division)
5. Processor (From Single Cycle Datapath to pipelined)
6. Memory (Locality and Hierarchy)

Also I heard rumors that the course will switch from MIPS to ARM in 1-2 years? Maybe MIPS is out of date now.
comment_teaching:
Cindy's teaching is nice and clear.

She usually starts the lecture with a 20min review of previous lecture and she uses ipad to illustrate core ideas clearly. She also responds fast to the questions in the chat.

Only downside is that it seems the Wifi connection in her office is not so good, so the Zoom teaching often lags. But this shouldn't be a problem if we resume F2F.

I didn't go to Alex's lectures but I heard from my freinds that his teaching was decently good as well. Both instructors deserves an A for "teaching".

But the TAs, on the other hand, differs quite greatly. Initially we had IA Bing Yen who was very helpful and responsible. But later due to some unknown reasons (rumors say he was fired or whatever), he disappeared from the course. And not all the PG TAs were really helpful. One example is the TA responsible for the project, despite he offered an optional introduction tutorial for the project at the beginning, but he barely replies to any posts about the project on Piazza. It was Alex who responded to most questions on Piazza eventually, but shouldn't that be the TA's job? And also only a few PG TAs can speak proper English, most of them have a very strong accent.
comment_workload:

The MIPS programming project needs one whole day or maybe slightly more depending on progress.

However this course is pretty competitive and losing just a few marks can cause a subgrade. So do it's probably necessary to revise very cearfully for the exams.

comment_teaching:
\\ Lecture in Recording Form //

Alex's teaching style is perfect if you
watch his lecture recording only.

In the recording, you can adjust
the timeline and play speed as you like.

I set the speed to 1.25x and
skipped all the blowing water parts.

During the lecture, he directly draws
pictures and do calculations on the slide.

As a result,
he often made the notes messy.

You may pause and try to digest his
notes in the recording.

Also, he will review and try to summarize
the materials in the previous lecture.

If you are confident in what he is reviewing,
you may simply skip them since he is just
repeating what he said previously.

----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

\\ Lecture in Real-time Live //

Biased Comment Erased :)

You may join his class during Add-drop
and see if you enjoy his real-time teaching.

----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

\\ Overall Comment //

--- [ 1. Lecture ] ---
Join Real-time Live or Watch Recording.

Never skip them unless you can digest
ALL details in the Lecture Notes.

--- [ 2. Tutorial ] ---
Self-study is recommended.

If you found the topic too difficult.
Watch the recording or go to Piazza.

--- [ 3. Labs ] ---
Self-study if time allows
and you want to learn more.

Otherwise, just ignore most of the labs.
They are useless for HW and Exams.

** One important Lab **
"Debugging on MARS" is important for
debugging your MIPS HW & Project
as well as the ability of code tracing.

----- ----- ----- ----- ----- ----- -----

【整体看法】

Lecture 一定要去，
光看 Note 你会一脸懵逼的。
至于是 Alex 还是 Cindy 就见仁见智吧。

建议趁 Add-drop 有时间去体验
两位讲师的现场授课和录像授课，
然后决定跟哪位、去现场还是看录像。

Tutorial 可以不去，
但里面的练习一定要试着做。
对功课考试乃至概念理解都有帮助。

如果里面的总结和题实在看不懂，
就看录像听 TA 讲解，或者去 Piazza 问。

如果你只关心成绩的话，
Lab 在大部分情况下根本不用去。

注：
其他 Lab 都能跳过，但有一个 Lab 除外。
那就是「Debugging on MARS」

学会怎么在 MARS 去 Debug 不但可以
提高你做 MIPS 作业时的效率，还能
训练你 Code Tracing 的能力。

comment_teaching:
Cindy explains everything clearly and logically. She usually conducts a little review before starting the content of the next lecture, which is of great help. You can always get a pretty good overview of the course content if you follow her teaching, and this saves a lot of time after class. What's more, she is always there to take questions after the lecture, and I have really learned a lot by discussing with her.
Nicko is in charge of the tutorial and he is a good guy. Although he is always afraid of not being able to finish the content LOL, he explains things clearly and they are echoing with the lectures.
For labs, however, I feel bad about it since I skipped most of them, as the TA does not have the best accent in the world, which makes me sleepy all the time :( I would say it is just fine to self-study the lab contents.
comment_workload:
Not a heavy workload, but it is not light-hearted either.
The past papers help a lot btw. Please remember to spend time on them
comment_grading:
4 Homework 15%
Midterm 30%
Project 15%
Final 40%

Homework is not hard at all, as you can find similar examples in lecture slides as well as tutorials. The tutorial is of great help to the problem-solving ability and a rather good review. For labs, I skipped some of them but some of the content covered by the lab may still be tested in the exam...(e.g. register file-related circuit stuff).
The project is not hard to comprehend. I would say it's just like a COMP1021 Lab in terms of difficulty. However since everything is in MIPS assembly, you should better develop good programming habits (e.g. saving $s register in the stack, etc), or it will be EXTREMELY PAINFUL to debug... It was truly a desperate experience for me hehe
The grading is actually getting better and better compared with previous years. According to Cindy, it was 15% A in the past but they manage to keep it expanding

comment_teaching:
Cindy is a good lecturer, concepts are explained pretty clearly and the examples given are also very sufficient. However, she does like to use hand drawn notes to elaborate the material, meaning that you need to pay full attention in the class otherwise you WILL miss something.

Lab and tutorial sessions do not require attendance, and don't relate much to the class content since the calculations and steps are already demonstrated in the lectures.
comment_workload:
For a 4 credit course it's low.

The homework assignments takes 1-2 hours to complete, and the programming assignment is not difficult as a lot of the given code can be reused to complete the game.

For the exams more time should be spent on doing the past papers and memorizing the latter half of the course as it involves a lot more memorization.
comment_grading:
Pretty standard grading.

4 Homeworks (15%): 5 questions or so each, similar to exam formats, difficulty is acceptable other than the last one. It is easy to make careless mistakes, so doublecheck your answer with a friend!

Programming project (15%): Done entirely in MIPS (a custom version of MIPS is used for this) where you finish a functions in a simple game and make a flowchart explaining the game functions, difficulty is pretty low, referencing how the given functions work is very helpful in finishing the project.

Midterm (30%): MC questions, and then long questions, doing the past papers is important since the questions are very similar.

Final (40%): Same as midterm, emphasis on the course materials taught after the midterm, more challenging than the midterm due to the difficulty of the material.

Ended up at mean, and got a B-.

comment_teaching:
Alex's teaching is generally good and passionate. Sometimes he even shows us some ways to relax before the start of the lesson, e.g. listen to studying music.
Even he teaches slowly, he makes sure we understand what is happening before going to the next topic.
comment_workload:
For homework, it is generally easy to handle. It is fine as long as you make reference to the lecture notes.

The project is also easy to handle if you have deep programming experience. Better if you attempt to convert codes into MIPS code.

However, learning MIPS is actually not an easy thing. After all, MIPS is a lower-level programming language, it is not user-friendly compare to higher-level languages like Python and C++. Without much experience, it can make students struggle to write an iteration statement in MIPS.

The assessments are different this semester. In this semester, it becomes more "MC-question orientated". The MCQ in midterm has moderate difficulty, but not the final (They are pretty hard despite it has only 4 choices).
With that aside, the difficulty of the quiz is generally fair.

comment_content:
More focus on the hardware side on things, such as the internal representation of integers and floats in computers, assembly language, and the CPU.

People say this is a memorization-heavy course, but I find that you just need to have an understanding of the big picture, because you can include the minute details in your epic cheat sheet and refer to it during the exam.
comment_teaching:
Cindy is a great professor. She explains all the concepts clearly. I find that I understand the materials very well after her lectures.

However, she talks very slowly, so I skipped all her lectures and watched the videos at 2.5x speed.

Sometimes she will miss your emails and forget to reply, but you can show up to her Q&A session and ask your question via zoom.
comment_workload:
The workload is quite manageable.

There were 4 homework, around 2 hours each.

The project took around 3 hours.

The project bonus part took me a whole day, because the TA who wrote the java emulator is a war criminal and I spend hours making sense of the code.

(The base portion of the project is in assembly but you need to modify the MIPS emulator in java for the bonus part)

Exams require around 3 days of revision each. Doing lots of past papers is necessary.
comment_grading:
The grading is not as bad as the others say, and is actually quite good.

Project 15%
Homework 15%
Midterm 30%
Final 40%

I got full marks for the project and homework, 0.5 bonus points in the project, and +1.2 SD for both midterms and finals.
The overall course grade was 93.5 -> A+

The exams are very straightforward. No tricky questions. Everything in the exam was covered in the lectures. If you review the materials thoroughly, you can probably score at least 80%. The only reasons you can have points deducted are either because you ran out of time (the exams were one-way-forward) or careless mistakes. Since you can't go back to previous questions in the exam, you may be tempted to triple-check your answers, but don't. You will end up running out of time.

I recommend reserving 3 days for revision for the midterms and finals. Moreover, the exams were open-book, but only printed materials are allowed, so a good cheat sheet will help a lot.

You can get up to 5 points from your project bonus. That's 5 points to the overall course grade, not the project grade, so it is totally worth it. You need to submit a proposal first, but there is no penalty if you can't finish what you propose or decide to work on something outside your proposal, so you can propose as many items as you want. The final bonus grade depends on what you end up being able to implement.

comment_content:
This course covers low-level computing, such as how computers store signed numbers, MIPS assembly language and how a basic processor works.

1. Introduction to Digital Logic
    • Logic gates
    • Combinational & sequential logic circuits
    • Boolean algebra & K-map
2. Data Representation
    • Signed & unsigned integers (2's complement)
    • Signed & unsigned floats (IEEE754)
3. MIPS Assembly Language
    • Arithmetic operations
    • Control flow statements & functions
    • Instruction formats
4. Arithmetic for Computers
    • Arithmetic Logic Unit
    • Integer Multiplication & Division
5. MIPS Processor
    • Single-cycle datapath & control
    • Pipelined Processor
6. Memory Hierarchy
    • Principle of Locality
    • Cache

This course materials are well prepared but you need lots of memorization. The MIPS processor is probably the most difficult chapter in the entire course. Luckily since the semester is in online mode, the mid-term and final are open book and open notes so I don't need to memorize.
comment_teaching:
Professor Lam is a very passionate lecturer and he is willing to interact with students and answer questions. His English is also very fluent and sometimes he talks too fast.

However, I'm not a fan of his teaching style. Firstly, he makes extremely messy notes while teaching (which he acknowledges this problem lol). He would put many meaningless circles and lines across the slide he is explaining and turns the entire slide into a graffiti. It's very easy to get lost when you are trying to figure out which part of the slide he's teaching.

Also, he sometimes spends excessive amount of time (eg. half an hour) to revise the last lecture's content. After realizing that, he would then skip through slides and explanations to catch up the teaching progress.

I highly recommend taking Li Xin's session since her teaching is highly organized and well-explained.
comment_workload:
I find the workload acceptable.

The homework is not too time consuming and each homework can be completed within a day. The project is a little bit time consuming since you need to program in assembly. The most time consuming part is debugging your assembly program, in which a single bug could take hours to find.

Since this semester is online-mode and the exams are open notes, it saves so much time memorizing the lecture notes and relief the workload for quite a bit.
